title: Applications & Analysis
lead: How the Basic Structure Doctrine is tested, applied and debated — from the width and identity tests to the Ninth Schedule and constitutional morality.

items.title: The Identity Test
bullets: Does the amendment change the identity of the Constitution so fundamentally that ==the document that emerges is not the same Constitution==? If yes, the amendment is void.
[[L Chandra Kumar (1997)]]: examine the feature's ==place in the constitutional scheme==, its object and purpose, and the consequences of denying it.

[[M Nagaraj (2006)]] distinguished ==constitutional axioms== (secularism, federalism) from ordinary legal rules (catch-up rule, consequential seniority) — altering the latter does not destroy the basic structure.

items.title: The Width Test
bullets: What is the ==true breadth of the amendment's impact== — does its reach transgress the basic structure?

Nagaraj upheld amendments to ==Art 16(4A)/(4B)== (reservation in promotions) but imposed BSD-derived conditions: ==quantifiable data on backwardness, inadequacy of representation, administrative efficiency==.
[[Jarnail Singh v. Lachhmi Narain Gupta (2018)]] removed the ==backwardness condition== for SCs/STs, retaining the other two.

items.title: Abrogation vs Abridgment
bullets: An amendment may ==curtail or regulate== a feature (permissible abridgement) but cannot ==destroy or abrogate== it.
The SC looks at ==degree of damage==: modified → survives; essence eliminated → falls.

items.title: The Rights Scrutiny Test
bullets: If the ==essence of Arts 14, 19 or 21== is destroyed, a law cannot be saved merely by placement in the ==Ninth Schedule==.
A concrete ==rights-impact analysis==, not just an abstract BSD inquiry.

blocks.text: ==Modification v. Damage==: not every amendment affecting a basic feature is invalid — it must ==damage, destroy, abrogate or emasculate== the basic structure. The width test is about ==scope of power used==; the identity test is about ==qualitative transformation== of the constitutional order.

sections.title: Where the Doctrine Has Been Applied

items.title: Judicial Review
bullets: [[Minerva Mills (1980)]] struck down 42nd CA clauses excluding court review of amendments.
[[L Chandra Kumar (1997)]]: review under ==Arts 226/227 and 32== is basic structure — tribunals supplement, ==never replace==, constitutional courts.

items.title: Democracy & Elections
bullets: [[Kihoto Hollohan (1993)]]: democracy, rule of law, ==free & fair elections== are basic features.
[[Indira Gandhi v. Raj Narain (1975)]]: election of high offices cannot be placed ==beyond judicial scrutiny==.

items.title: Ninth Schedule
bullets: [[Waman Rao (1981)]] and [[Bhim Singhji (1981)]]: even redistributive legislation cannot annihilate basic structure via ==blanket immunisation==.
[[IR Coelho (2007)]]: post-1973 IX Schedule laws tested against FRs that are themselves basic structure — especially where ==‘rights-destroying’== rather than merely rights-limiting.

items.title: FRs & DPSPs in Harmony
bullets: [[Minerva Mills (1980)]]: FRs protect ==liberty and dignity==; DPSPs promote ==social and economic justice== — their ==harmony and balance== is itself basic structure.

items.title: Secularism & Federalism
bullets: [[SR Bommai (1994)]]: both are basic features; anti-secular state governments can be dismissed but ==Presidential Proclamations under Art 356 need material== — judicial check over a previously non-justiciable domain.

items.title: Independence of the Judiciary
bullets: [[Madras Bar Association (2015)]] (tribunals) and the [[NJAC Case (2015)]] (appointments): judicial independence and separation of powers used to strike down changes threatening ==court autonomy==.

items.title: Socialism & Property
bullets: [[Property Owners Association v. State of MH (2024)]]: Art 31C stands as settled by Kesavananda/Minerva; ==not all private property is ‘material resources of the community’== under Art 39(b) — protecting property rights without reinstating Art 31.

items.title: Affirmative Impacts
bullets: ==Judicial review constitutionalised==: Arts 32 and 226 declared inviolable basic structure — [[L Chandra Kumar (1997)]] — self-perpetuating as an institution.
==Two-tier review==: ordinary Art 13 review of legislation + ==BSD meta-review== of amendments themselves — a doctrinal leap.
==First application==, [[Indira Gandhi (1975)]]: even the most consequential political decisions can be undone.
==Rights trinity==: [[Maneka Gandhi (1978)]] interlocked Arts 14, 19, 21 — procedure affecting life & liberty must be ==fair, just and reasonable==.
==Electoral integrity==: [[Kihoto Hollohan (1993)]] upheld the Tenth Schedule but preserved judicial review.

items.title: Critical Impacts
bullets: ==NJAC controversy==: a near-unanimous amendment ratified by states was invalidated by an unelected court — the ==counter-majoritarian difficulty== at its starkest.
==Vagueness==: undefined scope of ‘basic features’ breeds discretion and perpetual uncertainty.
==Judicial encroachment==: [[Vishaka (1997)]] guidelines show courts drifting into legislative territory.
==Policy disruption==: BSD-adjacent interventions have stalled infrastructure clearances, the ==farm laws (stay, 2021)== and reorganisations.

blocks.text: Normatively, basic structure transforms judicial review from mere legality control into ==‘constitutional guardianship’== — with all the attendant tension between ==democracy and juristocracy==.

sections.title: BSD as Democracy's Bulwark — and Its Challenges

items.title: Bulwark
bullets: ==The Emergency test==: Kesavananda is credited with keeping India a democracy in the 1970s; [[Minerva Mills (1980)]] striking down Arts 368(4)/(5) was the ==constitutional immune response== to self-entrenchment.

==Free & fair elections== as structural guarantee → built on by [[ADR v. UoI (2002)]] (candidate disclosure), [[Lily Thomas (2013)]] (disqualification of convicted legislators), [[Electoral Bonds Case (2024)]].
==Secularism & pluralism==: [[SR Bommai (1994)]] — a check on sectarian governance.
==Federalism== as democratic decentralisation — the Centre cannot amend India into a unitary state.
==Multi-party democracy==: [[Kuldip Nayar (2006)]] protects political pluralism from majoritarian entrenchment.

items.title: Challenges
bullets: ==Counter-majoritarianism institutionalised== — unelected judges striking down amendments of elected majorities.
==Constitutional freezing==: the collegium — itself judge-made — rendered effectively unamendable (NJAC).
==Ambiguity as a power tool==: an expanding portfolio of judicial veto points; the ==‘abstraction problem’== risks constitutionalising personal views.

==Comparative view==: Germany's eternity clauses (==Art 79(3) Grundgesetz==) are textual; South Africa's amendments are tiered; India's BSD is ==entirely judge-made== — flexible but exposed to charges of self-empowerment.

blocks.text: BSD can be defended as ==‘militant constitutionalism’== necessary amid weak party constraints and majoritarian excess — but it demands ==judicial self-restraint== and clear reasoning to remain democratically defensible.

sections.title: The First Amendment & the Ninth Schedule

items: The ==First Amendment (1951)== is a classic case of Parliament recalibrating rights for socio-economic reform — and the later BSD ==retroactively disciplining== that attempt. Triggered by [[Champakam Dorairajan (1951)]], [[Romesh Thappar (1950)]] and [[Motilal (1951)]].

==Art 19 changes==: narrowed free speech by adding ‘reasonable restrictions’ (public order, foreign relations) — responding to Romesh Thappar and [[Brij Bhushan]] which had robustly protected press freedom.
==Arts 31A & 31B + IX Schedule==: validated 13 state land-reform laws retrospectively — even statutes already held unconstitutional would be deemed valid once listed.
==Democratic legitimacy issue==: passed by the ==Provisional Parliament== elected on a limited franchise.

The Schedule began as a ==social revolution device== (protecting land reform) but became a ==constitutional immunity device== — corrected through Waman Rao's ==24 April 1973 cut-off== and Coelho's rights scrutiny.

head: Stage
Position of IX Schedule laws
rows: All Ninth Schedule laws ==immune== from FR challenge.
Immunity questioned but not definitively resolved.
Laws added ==before 24 April 1973== immune; those added after open to BSD challenge.
Post-24 April 1973 laws challengeable on basic structure ==AND on Arts 14, 15, 19, 21 specifically==.

blocks.text: The Ninth Schedule is ==not a constitutional black hole== — it is protected only so long as it does not become a device to ==destroy constitutional identity==.

items.title: Minerva Mills (1980)
bullets: The ==42nd CA (1976)== — a ‘mini Constitution’ amending 54 provisions during the Emergency — sought to reverse Kesavananda.
Struck down ==Arts 368(4) and 368(5)==; ==limited amending power is itself a basic feature==.
Art 31C restored to pre-42nd position; ==FR–DPSP harmony== affirmed. Justice Bhagwati's dissent stressed DPSPs' socio-economic importance.

items.title: Waman Rao (1981)
bullets: Set the ==24 April 1973 cut-off== (date of Kesavananda): earlier IX Schedule laws protected; later ones open to challenge.
A pragmatic balance: avoiding ==retroactive chaos== in settled land reforms while denying permanent immunisation.

items.title: I.R. Coelho (2007)
bullets: ==9-judge bench== confirmed Waman Rao's line.
Post-1973 IX Schedule laws challengeable on BSD ==and Arts 14, 15, 19, 21== — only ==‘fictional immunity’== remains in Art 31B.
Reaffirmed ==rule of law and separation of powers== as basic features.

blocks.text: The trilogy is the ==jurisprudential maturation== of the BSD: creation (Kesavananda) → defence against reversal (Minerva Mills) → temporal calibration (Waman Rao) → comprehensive final form (Coelho).

items: ==Classic position==: Kesavananda directed the doctrine exclusively at the ==amending power== under Art 368.
[[Madras Bar Association v. UoI (2015)]]: ==ordinary laws too== cannot violate the basic structure — a doctrinal leap.
[[Anjum Qadri v. UoI (2024)]] (UP Madrassa): the test is ==rigorous== — one must specifically demonstrate how an ordinary law violates the basic structure; vague invocation fails.

head: Constitutional amendment
Ordinary legislation
rows: Tested under ==Art 368 and the basic structure doctrine==.
Tested mainly for ==legislative competence==, Fundamental Rights and other constitutional limitations.
Parliament/State legislature acts in ordinary ==legislative capacity==.

items: ==Constitutional text== — Parliament can amend, subject to BSD
==Basic structure== — constrains both constituent and ordinary legislative power
==Ordinary laws== — must conform to Part III (Art 13) and must not damage basic structural features
BSD is the ==juridical operationalisation of constitutional morality== — the doctrine provides the mechanism; morality provides the normative content of what counts as ‘basic’.
Amendments are ==solemn moral choices== about the kind of polity India wishes to be — when they conflict with the Constitution's moral architecture (42nd CA, 39th CA), BSD corrects them.

==The Preamble as moral source==: from not part of the Constitution ([[Berubari Union (1960)]]) to its integral source ([[Kesavananda (1973)]]) — its text can be amended, but its objectives, having acquired BSD status, ==cannot be destroyed==.
==Transformative constitutionalism==: the judiciary drove social revolution through BSD and PIL — but faces growing criticism of ==overreach== into legislative and executive domains.

blocks.text: Its ultimate value lies in the assurance it gives every citizen that the Constitution's promise of justice, liberty, equality, and fraternity — written into the Preamble with profound deliberation — cannot be erased by any arithmetic of political majority, however large.
